The Problem

The maker journey collapses at the BOM stage.

Every day Filipino makers, hobbyists, and engineering students get inspired by a YouTube build or a forum parts list. The moment they try to buy it, the journey breaks down — one part at a time.

  • Identify each component by name and exact spec
  • Search for it across supplier catalogs and physical stores
  • Verify stock availability and correct packaging quantities
  • Find alternatives when preferred components are out of stock
  • Repeat this for every single part in the list

Project abandonment is the norm, not the exception.

Lost sales for suppliers like Circuit Rocks. Discouraged hobbyists who never finish their builds. A maker community growing in interest but shrinking in completion rate — local stock data is offline, outdated, or scattered across Facebook messages.

The Solution

Two complementary paths. One in-stock cart.

CircuitForge separates curation from selection. Circuit Rocks owns the expert/inventory layer — catalog, stock status, kits, verified alternatives. The maker, with the AI doing the heavy lifting, only makes the final decision.

The Hero

AI Build Assistant

Drop in inspiration in any form — a schematic/BOM photo or free text. The assistant (Groq, with a Gemini vision fallback) proposes a parts list, matches every line to the live catalog, and auto-swaps out-of-stock items for verified alternatives. Builds are saved and shareable by link.

Guaranteed Path

Curated Project Kits

For makers who want a sure thing, Circuit Rocks experts pre-curate complete projects — a built variant plus a verified Bill of Materials — that drop into the cart with a single "Add all to cart". Arduino Line Follower, DIY Bluetooth Speaker, Raspberry Pi Weather Station, and more.

1

Bring your build

Upload a schematic / parts-list photo, or just describe it in chat — or pick a curated Project Kit.

2

Let it resolve

The AI matches each part to verified, in-stock circuit.rocks components, auto-swapping out-of-stock items.

3

Check out

One click adds the resolved parts to your cart. Finish with delivery or in-store pickup.

User Journey

From inspiration to placed order — both lanes.

The maker lane (top) runs on the AI assistant as its spine. The always-on Circuit Rocks admin lane (bottom) curates the catalog, kits, and multi-warehouse stock — so every maker step above resolves against live availability.

FIG.01 — CircuitForge user journeymaker lane · admin lane

Not a wireframe — a working full-stack app.

The complete journey from inspiration to a placed order is live in the prototype. A few of the screens:

AI Build Chat — resolved build card

Every suggested part matched against the live catalog. In-stock parts are badged; anything unmatched is flagged with a reason so nothing silently disappears. Estimated total and a one-click Add all to cart sit right on the card.

/build · conversation

Build Assistant

Conversational, not a form

I want to build an LED cube

Fun project! What size are you thinking — a small 4×4×4, or a larger 8×8×8?

BRAINSTORMGRILL MEIMPECCABLE

Pressure-tested before it resolves

The assistant interviews the idea — brainstorm, grill, and impeccable modes refine the build until the parts list is right.

/projects · kit → cart

Curated kit → one-click cart

A built variant plus a verified BOM, each line backed by live inventory. The whole kit consolidates into a single checkout-ready cart with a running subtotal.

/login · accounts (live screen)

Customer accounts

Email + password or Google OAuth. Saved builds, delivery addresses, and full order history with status tracking — guests get a cookie-backed cart and build history too.

Every screen in the prototype

Maker (Customer) Experience

  • HomeFeatured products, curated project rails, and a clear path into the build assistant.
  • Product DetailImage gallery, variant/option pickers, live stock status, add-to-cart.
  • Projects Gallery & Project DetailCurated kits with in-stock badges and one-click "Add all to cart".
  • AI Build ChatDescribe / upload an image; the assistant replies with an inline resolved-build card (matched parts, stock status, auto-swapped alternatives).
  • Build Detail & My BuildsA shareable resolved build with an alternates picker, plus a history of saved builds (guest or signed-in).
  • Cart Drawer & 3-Step CheckoutContact → delivery/pickup (with PH address cascade) → payment method + proof upload → order confirmation.
  • AccountOrder history with status tracking, saved addresses, and profile.

Admin (Circuit Rocks) Experience

  • DashboardOrder, revenue, and inventory-alert metrics.
  • Products, Project Kits, Inventory, OrdersCatalog and kit curation, per-warehouse stock management, and the order fulfillment workflow (verify payment → ship → deliver).
  • Manufacturing & LogisticsBOMs, build orders, raw materials, warehouses, branches, stock transfers, and payment-method configuration.
The maker flow follows a discovery-to-purchase path with the AI assistant as the spine: bring inspiration in any form → assistant resolves to in-stock parts → one decision (keep or swap an alternative) → checkout. This keeps cognitive load on the maker to a minimum.
Features & Modules

What's in the box.

User

AI Build Assistant

A chat assistant that turns inspiration into parts. Accepts text or an uploaded image (schematic/BOM photo). Groq primary + Gemini vision fallback, with brainstorm / grill / impeccable modes.

User

Smart Parts Matching

Every suggested part matched against the live circuit.rocks catalog. Out-of-stock parts auto-swapped for verified in-stock alternatives; unmatched parts flagged with the reason so nothing silently disappears.

User

Saved & Shareable Builds

Every resolved build is persisted — signed-in users and guests (via cookie) — and viewable at a shareable link, so a teammate sees the same parts with current stock and adds them independently.

Admin + User

Project Catalog (Curated Kits)

Admins publish curated project kits — a built variant plus a verified BOM, with estimated cost and an in-stock badge. Users browse the gallery and open any kit.

Admin

Curated Parts List (BOM)

For each kit, admins define a complete Bill of Materials from raw materials and components, backed by live inventory. Only one BOM is active per variant at a time.

User

Ready-to-Cart Output

Both the AI build and any curated kit produce a one-click "Add (all) to cart" — a consolidated, checkout-ready cart with per-item stock status and a total cost estimate.

Admin + User

Inventory Status Layer

Each component surfaced as In Stock, Low Stock, or Out of Stock. The platform automatically routes around out-of-stock items via alternatives, preventing dead ends.

User

Storefront & Cart

Full product browsing (catalog, detail with variant/option pickers), a persistent guest or signed-in cart, and a 3-step checkout with delivery or in-store pickup, PH address cascade (region → province → city → barangay), and manual payment-proof upload.

User

Customer Accounts

Register / login (email + password, plus Google OAuth), saved delivery addresses, profile, and full order history with status tracking.

Admin

Admin Dashboard

Central control panel with metrics (orders, revenue, inventory alerts) and management of products, kits, inventory, and orders.

Admin

Component Database

A searchable catalog of all Circuit Rocks products and variants, plus tracked raw materials, with specs, categories, SKUs, and cross-reference data for alternatives.

Admin

Multi-Warehouse Inventory & Transfers

Per-warehouse stock buckets with global rollups, reorder points, branch↔warehouse mapping (many-to-many), and inter-warehouse stock transfers with a full lifecycle.

Admin

In-house Manufacturing

Build orders consume raw materials against a BOM to produce finished/kit variants, with a draft → planned → in-progress → completed lifecycle.

Admin

Order Fulfillment

Verify uploaded payment proof, then move orders through ship → deliver, with cancel support. Configurable manual payment methods (with QR codes).

Admin + User

Difficulty & Category Tags

Products and projects carry category tags (and, where set, difficulty) so makers find builds appropriate to their skill. (Difficulty tagging is partially surfaced in the UI.)

Who It's For

Two customers. Both have to be engaged.

CircuitForge serves two distinct groups in the Filipino maker ecosystem — the platform only works when the admin side (Circuit Rocks) and the user side (makers) are both in.

Target customer

Primary Customer
Circuit Rocks — the electronics supplier that powers the platform. It converts browsing makers into direct, project-complete purchases. Every template curated and every build resolved is a sales funnel for its component catalog.
End Users
Filipino makers, electronics hobbyists, engineering students, and DIY enthusiasts who discover project tutorials and need a fast, reliable way to source the components locally.
Location
Circuit Rocks' physical and online presence as the primary fulfillment channel.
Behavior
Regularly consumes YouTube tutorials, Instructables, and Hackster.io content. Buys components online (Shopee, Lazada) or in-store. Active in maker communities on Facebook, Reddit, and Discord.
Pain Point
Spends hours per project just sourcing components. Frequently abandons builds over out-of-stock parts or no local alternative. Relies on informal Facebook messages to suppliers to check availability.
Purchase Trigger
Discovers a project tutorial they want to build — the inspiration moment. CircuitForge captures the user at this exact point and converts it before motivation fades.

User persona

MS
Miguel "Migs" Santos
21 · 3rd-year EE student · Quezon City, Metro Manila
Role
3rd-year Electronics Engineering student; weekend hobbyist and aspiring robotics builder.
Tech Comfort
High — codes in Arduino/Python, follows YouTube and Hackster.io builds, active in a campus robotics club and several maker Facebook groups.
Goals
Finish the projects he gets excited about; source the right parts quickly and locally; stay within a student budget; learn by building, not by hunting for components.
Frustrations
Spends hours translating a tutorial into a buyable parts list; finds half the parts out of stock with no obvious substitute; ends up messaging multiple Facebook sellers; often loses momentum and abandons the build entirely.
Behavior
Discovers a build on YouTube → screenshots the parts list or schematic → tries to find each part on Shopee/Lazada or in-store → gives up when stock is unclear.
How It Helps
Migs uploads the schematic photo into the build assistant. CircuitForge resolves it into a verified, in-stock cart of circuit.rocks parts in seconds — auto-swapping anything out of stock — and he checks out with in-store pickup near campus. The build that would have died at the BOM stage gets finished.
Prototype Stack

How it's built.

Frontend
React 19 + Vite 8 client-side SPA — TanStack Router & Query, Zod, react-hook-form, Axios, Recharts, Tailwind CSS 4.
Backend
NestJS 11 REST, domain modules + Prisma 7 ORM.
Database
PostgreSQL (dev: Docker container circuit-pg, port 5433).
Auth
Passport — Local + JWT + Google OAuth 2.0; 15-min access + hashed refresh in a Session table; httpOnly cr_at / cr_rt cookies.
AI Layer
Groq (primary — Llama 3.3 70B) + Google Gemini (fallback — 2.5 Flash, vision). Powers the assistant and the parts matcher.
Hosting
Local development / Docker Compose (Postgres). Not yet deployed to a public host.
Repository
github.com/CodedCypher/C-C
Live URL
n/a (local development).

Use of AI in the Project

Disclosed in full.

The following AI tool was used during development of CircuitForge. Its use is disclosed in accordance with the hackathon rules and submission guidelines.

ClaudeAnthropic · dev tooling
Full development — architecture, frontend SPA, NestJS backend, AI build-assistant integration, debugging, and documentation.

AI inside the product (runtime). CircuitForge's build assistant and parts matcher are themselves AI-powered, using Groq (primary) and Google Gemini (fallback) to interpret maker inspiration and resolve it into in-stock parts. This is a product feature, distinct from the development tooling above.

All AI-generated content was reviewed, validated, and edited by the team before inclusion in any submission. Core decisions on product design, architecture, and presentation were made by team members.
